one_liner: A shoppable music video where every product is Black-owned.
search_doc:
A shoppable music video where every product is Black-owned.
Mechanisms: shoppable-music-video, celebrity-as-prop, purpose-driven-music, platform-ecosystem-mobilisation
Idea: Multihyphenate queen and Black entrepreneur Keke Palmer championed Black-owned businesses and products with a re-creation of the '90s club classic '100% Pure Love' by Crystal Waters dubbed '100% Black-Owned,' lyrics that reinforced the pride and power of Black entrepreneurship. A shoppable music video, set to the song, featured 75 products and Black business owners nationwide. We also encouraged businesses to adopt the Black-owned business badge on Google Search and Maps, and created bespoke social content for business owners on Google's most visible channels.
Insight: We aim to increase awareness and visibility of Black-owned businesses, nationwide and year-round, by equipping them with the power of Google's product ecosystem, including Maps, Search, Ads space, and AI tools that can supercharge their marketing.

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR MUSIC/BRONZE/Black-Owned Friday - Google (Google, Mountain View _ Google Brand Studio, San Francisco).jpg

extracted.context: Google's Black-Owned Friday campaign originated in 2020 to uplift Black-owned businesses disproportionately affected by the pandemic. This initiative remains relevant in its 4th year; only 5% of holiday shopping revenue is generated at Black-owned businesses, and 13% of Americans say they regularly shop at them.
extracted.insight: We aim to increase awareness and visibility of Black-owned businesses, nationwide and year-round, by equipping them with the power of Google's product ecosystem, including Maps, Search, Ads space, and AI tools that can supercharge their marketing.
extracted.idea: Multihyphenate queen and Black entrepreneur Keke Palmer championed Black-owned businesses and products with a re-creation of the '90s club classic '100% Pure Love' by Crystal Waters dubbed '100% Black-Owned,' lyrics that reinforced the pride and power of Black entrepreneurship. A shoppable music video, set to the song, featured 75 products and Black business owners nationwide. We also encouraged businesses to adopt the Black-owned business badge on Google Search and Maps, and created bespoke social content for business owners on Google's most visible channels.
execution: Shoppable music video featuring Keke Palmer and 75 Black-owned products
Re-recording of '100% Pure Love' as '100% Black-Owned'
Soundtrack released on streaming services
Black-owned business badge on Google Search and Maps
Microsite with e-commerce training and Product Studio AI tools
Homepage shoutout on Google.com and Google's social channels
Dynamic OOH rotation in NYC and LA spotlighting Black-owned businesses near the billboards
Broadcast flight during Black Friday weekend
metrics: 40+ positive press mentions in major outlets

inferred.one_liner: A shoppable music video where every product is Black-owned.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Re-recorded a '90s club anthem as a Keke Palmer-fronted shoppable music video for 75 Black-owned businesses, scaling Google's ecosystem (Search, Maps, OOH) behind the moment for 374M impressions and 99% positive sentiment.

visual.style_description: Editorial dark-grey board with a moody Keke Palmer hero portrait singing into a vintage mic; dense left and right text columns explain strategy and ecosystem, bold metric callouts run across the top, a 'Shop The Video' product grid and execution thumbnails sit on the right and bottom.

idea.rationale: Re-recording Crystal Waters's '90s club classic '100% Pure Love' as '100% Black-Owned' with Keke Palmer and embedding 75 Black-owned products into a shoppable music video lands a familiar shoppable-video mechanic with a music-anthem twist. The lyric retrofit is on-brief and culturally legible, but the underlying mechanic (shoppable music video) and the celebrity-led format are now well-established rather than category-redefining. The idea is the fourth annual iteration of Black-Owned Friday, which limits its claim to originality.

strategy.rationale: Google is the world's dominant search and ad-platform company whose stated ecosystem mission includes equipping small businesses with discovery and marketing tools, so mobilising Search, Maps, Ads space and Product Studio AI behind Black-owned businesses is direct ecosystem-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The context is data-backed (5% of holiday shopping at Black-owned businesses, 13% of Americans regularly shop at them) and the four-year programme continuity gives the cause genuine institutional commitment. Soft spot: insight is positioned as visibility/awareness rather than a sharper diagnosis of why those numbers are stuck.

execution.rationale: Orchestration spans music video, soundtrack streaming release, business-badge activation across Search and Maps, an e-commerce-training microsite with AI Product Studio tools, dynamic OOH near featured businesses in NYC/LA, broadcast flight during Black Friday weekend, and Google.com homepage placement. Channel choice is appropriately ecosystem-wide — the campaign exploits Google's own surfaces as media. Phase orchestration is solid but conventional; no single channel choice carries surprise.

impact.rationale: 374M+ impressions and 14.6M video views are entrant-reported and unaudited reach metrics, and the doubling of social engagement (355K in 2023 vs 161K in 2022) is anchored to a real prior-year base, which gives it more credibility than a percentage alone. The 99% positive sentiment claim is unbased and reads as a soft signal. The implied business outcome — measurable lift in revenue or customer counts at Black-owned businesses — is not shown on the board, so impact rests on reach and sentiment rather than the commercial change the programme exists to drive.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the ecosystem-wide mobilisation of Google's surfaces behind Black-owned businesses is genuinely on-mission, but it is the fourth iteration of a now-familiar mechanic and the impact case rests on reach and sentiment rather than commercial lift.

one_liner: Beyonce drops a country album to break Verizon's network.
search_doc:
Beyonce drops a country album to break Verizon's network.
Mechanisms: celebrity-as-stress-test, super-bowl-stunt, album-launch-as-proof, cultural-moment-via-music
Idea: On Super Bowl night, we turned the most watched event of the year and biggest night in American culture into the biggest network test and music drop of all time.
Insight: A surprise Beyonce drop during the Super Bowl is the only cultural moment big enough to simultaneously stress-test a network and break the internet — proving Verizon can handle both.

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR MUSIC/GOLD/Can_t B Broken - Verizon (Ogilvy New York).jpg

extracted.context: Verizon wanted to redefine Americana with her new country album and reach as wide of an audience as possible. And Verizon wanted to prove that America's most reliable network can handle anything and never be broken.
extracted.idea: On Super Bowl night, we turned the most watched event of the year and biggest night in American culture into the biggest network test and music drop of all time.
execution: Super Bowl commercial announcing Beyonce's new album
Album launch tied to a network stress test
Hit singles topping charts (Texas Hold 'Em, 16 Carriages)

inferred.insight: A surprise Beyonce drop during the Super Bowl is the only cultural moment big enough to simultaneously stress-test a network and break the internet — proving Verizon can handle both.
inferred.one_liner: Beyonce drops a country album to break Verizon's network.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Beyonce's Super Bowl Verizon spot doubled as the launch of her Cowboy Carter era, generating 29 billion impressions, two chart-topping singles and a 35% lift in Verizon sign-ups.

visual.style_description: Cinematic hero photograph of Beyonce in a red sequinned outfit holding a phone like a megaphone against billowing red fabric; bold white 'CAN'T B BROKEN' display type wraps around her; press logo strip at the top, metric callouts and small execution thumbnails below.

idea.rationale: Staging Beyonce's Cowboy Carter album drop as a real-time stress test of Verizon's network during the Super Bowl fuses a network-reliability claim with the single biggest music release moment of the year. The construction is elegant: the same act of an artist 'trying to break the internet' becomes the product demonstration. It is music-native because Verizon contributed to the album's launch architecture, not just rented a celebrity face.

strategy.rationale: Verizon is America's largest mobile-network operator whose category positioning hinges on reliability ('most reliable network'), so contracting the year's most-anticipated cultural drop to prove the network cannot be broken is direct mission-fit, not borrowed celebrity. The implicit insight that only a Beyonce-sized cultural moment is big enough to genuinely test a US network at Super Bowl scale is plausible, but the board does not cite traffic-load benchmarks to verify the network actually held. The brand-music authority claim rests on the deal itself rather than on prior music credentials.

execution.rationale: Orchestration phases run from the Super Bowl in-game commercial to the simultaneous album release, chart push of 'Texas Hold 'Em' and '16 Carriages' to #1, and earned-media saturation across music and business press. Channel choice fits the audience: broadcast for the launch moment, streaming for the drop, social and press for the amplification loop. The Super Bowl is the only US stage with the simultaneity required to land a stress-test narrative.

impact.rationale: The 35% lift in Verizon sign-ups is the right business outcome for a telco — a conversion metric, not a surrogate — though the absolute base for 35% of what is not disclosed on the board. The two #1 Billboard singles and #1 album position are externally verifiable music-industry outcomes, which strengthens the case. Claimed 29 billion impressions and 31% Super Bowl conversation share are entrant-reported and unaudited, but breadth of press across The New York Times, WSJ, Variety, Rolling Stone, Billboard and Forbes is a credible signal of cultural penetration.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because the brief 'prove America's most reliable network can handle anything' was answered by literally co-launching the year's biggest album on its biggest night, with two chart-topping singles and a sign-up lift as proof.

one_liner: Two Grammy winners donate a song to fight cancer.
search_doc:
Two Grammy winners donate a song to fight cancer.
Mechanisms: artist-collab-deep, song-as-fundraiser, cultural-anthem-recontextualisation, purpose-driven-music
Idea: 'Yo vengo a ofrecer mi corazon' is one of Latin America's iconic songs, composed by the world-renowned and award-winning Argentine singer Fito Paez. It was transformed into an alternative and emotional rendition, performed by none other than the famous Grammy-winning Peruvian singer Susana Baca. This rendition aimed to deeply resonate with the entire nation, to get inside the skin of its people, and raise awareness about the challenges many low-income families face in accessing cancer treatment.
Insight: Donation fatigue is broken when two beloved Grammy winners give the country a song instead of an ask — a gift of art becomes the most persuasive plea for solidarity.

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR MUSIC/SILVER/Cancer Before Cancer - Peruvian Cancer Foundation (Fahrenheit DDB Lima).webp

extracted.tagline: 2 Grammy winners donate their talent so a whole country donate against cancer.
extracted.context: Every year since 1969, the Peruvian Cancer Foundation has been seeking donations to assist thousands of low-income cancer patients across the country. However, each year, the fundraising efforts receive fewer donations, despite our various efforts to make it easier and more accessible for donors to contribute. That's why this year, we've decided to showcase the harsh reality that many patients endure to get the treatment they deeply need.
extracted.idea: 'Yo vengo a ofrecer mi corazon' is one of Latin America's iconic songs, composed by the world-renowned and award-winning Argentine singer Fito Paez. It was transformed into an alternative and emotional rendition, performed by none other than the famous Grammy-winning Peruvian singer Susana Baca. This rendition aimed to deeply resonate with the entire nation, to get inside the skin of its people, and raise awareness about the challenges many low-income families face in accessing cancer treatment.
execution: Reimagined version of Fito Paez classic 'Yo vengo a ofrecer mi corazon' performed by Susana Baca
Music film telling the true story of Peruvians who travel long distances for oncological treatment
Featured artists: Fito Paez (63 Grammy) and Susana Baca (63 Grammy)

inferred.insight: Donation fatigue is broken when two beloved Grammy winners give the country a song instead of an ask — a gift of art becomes the most persuasive plea for solidarity.
inferred.one_liner: Two Grammy winners donate a song to fight cancer.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Two Grammy-winning Latin American icons re-recorded a beloved anthem as a music film raising awareness and donations for low-income Peruvian cancer patients struggling to reach treatment.

visual.style_description: Cinematic dark-toned portrait diptych of Fito Paez and Susana Baca in performance, lit by warm spotlight; right column carries 'CONTEXT' and 'IDEA' text blocks in gold script, with a film-still strip and a small documentary photo of patients below.

idea.rationale: Recasting Fito Paez's Latin-American standard 'Yo vengo a ofrecer mi corazon' as a Susana Baca rendition transforms a beloved anthem from a love song into a literal donation appeal — the title itself ('I come to offer my heart') becomes a fundraising line. The recontextualisation idea is elegant and the artist pairing (Argentine composer + Peruvian Afro-Latin Grammy-winner) carries the cross-border solidarity message. The mechanic is familiar territory for cause-music but the specific song choice gives it a layer of cultural inevitability.

strategy.rationale: The Peruvian Cancer Foundation is a non-profit that has, since 1969, raised funds for low-income cancer patients across Peru, so a high-cultural donation appeal aligns directly with its decades-long mission rather than borrowing purpose. The context is data-backed by the operational reality that fundraising has been declining year-over-year despite easier donation tools, which is a verifiable institutional pain point. The under-the-skin emotional argument relies on the song's pre-existing meaning rather than fresh insight — credible, but more found than discovered.

execution.rationale: Two Grammy-winning artists genuinely donate their talent and the production runs as a music film telling true patient stories of long-distance oncological journeys — a craft execution rather than a celebrity stunt. Channels appropriate to the cause are implied (music release plus film), but the board does not detail orchestration phases, paid amplification, retail or partner activation beyond the song-and-film core. The absence of broadcaster, streaming or platform partner detail limits how much credit the orchestration can carry.

impact.rationale: The board carries no quantified impact data — no donation lift, no stream count, no reach, no behaviour-change benchmark — which is the most serious weakness for a Music entry, where streaming/chart/engagement evidence is the category's checklist item. The implied business outcome for a fundraising NGO is donations raised against baseline, and that specific number is conspicuously absent. The cultural weight of the two artists and the song is real but functions as input credibility, not outcome proof.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because two Grammy winners genuinely donating a Latin-American anthem to a low-income cancer cause is a credibly mission-fit gesture, but the impact case is unquantified and the orchestration is undocumented beyond the song-and-film core.

one_liner: A brand prints the erratum history forgot to publish.
search_doc:
A brand prints the erratum history forgot to publish.
Mechanisms: historical-record-correction, newspaper-erratum-as-medium, artist-restoration, purpose-driven-sponsorship, legacy-rewrite
Idea: As a brand that puts every people's journey, Johnnie Walker decided to right this historical wrong of Brazilian music. We decided to pay for it to show everyone who she really was. The first act was to buy two pages in Brazil's largest newspapers and create the longest 'erratum' ever printed: an entire career corrected through every chapter of Alaide's life, songs and recordings. After that, we realised that a new movement was set to honour Real historical icon at Carnegie Hall. And Alaide was left out, again. So, the brand made the official sponsor of her participation. Hands rising on the stage of Carnegie Hall and was the only artist to receive a standing ovation for more than 5 minutes. The next day, Alaide's life changed. She made headlines in all major media, was interviewed by the main TV and streaming programmes, won prestigious awards, and much more.
Insight: Being overlooked dimmed her entire career. That meant she never got the space she deserved in major media outlets and was left aside by the biggest shows and record companies. Why? Because she was Black, and a woman, and her heart, in black-woman style, kept on singing.

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR MUSIC/GRAND PRIX/Errata At 88 - Diageo (ALMAPBBDO, Sao Paulo).webp

extracted.tagline: Racism, elitism, sexism, and ageism erased Alaide Costa, the founding mother of Bossa Nova, from history. At 88, we put her back.
extracted.context: Although it was born in a country with tremendous racial diversity, in 1960s Brazil Bossa Nova was largely codified by white men. Alaide Costa, one of Brazil's greatest singers and one of the founding mothers of Bossa Nova, was almost erased.
extracted.insight: Being overlooked dimmed her entire career. That meant she never got the space she deserved in major media outlets and was left aside by the biggest shows and record companies. Why? Because she was Black, and a woman, and her heart, in black-woman style, kept on singing.
extracted.idea: As a brand that puts every people's journey, Johnnie Walker decided to right this historical wrong of Brazilian music. We decided to pay for it to show everyone who she really was. The first act was to buy two pages in Brazil's largest newspapers and create the longest 'erratum' ever printed: an entire career corrected through every chapter of Alaide's life, songs and recordings. After that, we realised that a new movement was set to honour Real historical icon at Carnegie Hall. And Alaide was left out, again. So, the brand made the official sponsor of her participation. Hands rising on the stage of Carnegie Hall and was the only artist to receive a standing ovation for more than 5 minutes. The next day, Alaide's life changed. She made headlines in all major media, was interviewed by the main TV and streaming programmes, won prestigious awards, and much more.
execution: Two-page erratum in Brazil's largest newspapers correcting Alaide Costa's history
Brand-funded sponsorship of her appearance at Carnegie Hall
Re-issue / restoration of her recordings and back catalogue
Earned media interviews across major TV and streaming programmes

inferred.one_liner: A brand prints the erratum history forgot to publish.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Repositioned Johnnie Walker as the leading and most-loved whiskey brand by rewriting the public record of a Black Brazilian Bossa Nova pioneer, restoring her career with measurable spikes in streams, fees and bookings.

visual.style_description: Dark cinematic case board with black-and-white archival portraits of Alaide Costa singing on stage layered with a bold yellow 'ERRATA at 88' logotype hero; left and right sides combine historical newspaper cut-outs, Carnegie Hall imagery and press logos in a dense editorial grid.

idea.rationale: Buying a two-page erratum in Brazil's biggest newspapers to literally correct the historical record of a Black Bossa Nova foundress is a reframe of music journalism itself — the medium of correction becomes the artistic gesture. The idea reads instantly: a brand admits, on behalf of a culture, that one of its founding mothers was erased, and then funds her belated coronation at Carnegie Hall. It is native to music because it intervenes inside the canon-formation machinery of records, press and live performance.

strategy.rationale: Johnnie Walker is Diageo's global Scotch whisky brand whose long-standing 'Keep Walking' / 'Every Journey' platform has framed individual progress as the brand mission, so funding the resumed journey of an 88-year-old Black woman is exact mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The insight — that 1960s Brazilian Bossa Nova was codified by white men and that Alaide Costa was structurally overlooked because she was Black and a woman — is a verifiable, named historical wrong rather than a soft cultural claim. The board does not benchmark Costa's pre-campaign obscurity with a number, which is the only Strategy soft spot.

execution.rationale: Orchestration runs from earned print (full-page newspaper erratum) to live spectacle (sponsoring her Carnegie Hall appearance and standing ovation) to catalogue restoration and earned TV/streaming interviews, so each phase amplifies the next inside music-native channels. Media choices are precise to the target: newspaper for the historical correction, Carnegie Hall for canon-entry, streaming for catalogue re-discovery, broadcast for cultural ratification. The yellow Johnnie Walker visual system anchors continuity across every touchpoint.

impact.rationale: Streaming and discovery lifts are individually large but expressed as percentages without an absolute base — +800% Spotify listeners, +747% Google searches and +318% YouTube clicks all leave 'percent of what?' open, so the magnitudes are directional rather than hard. The artist-economic outcomes — +200% fee and +500% performance invitations — are the right business proxies for an artist-restoration mission and read as concrete career change. Press across CNN, Vogue, Folha and Estadao is unaudited but the breadth across mainstream and culture outlets is a credible signal of national conversation.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins the Grand Prix because the brand bought a newspaper erratum and a Carnegie Hall seat to rewrite the historical record of Brazilian music itself, turning paid media into an act of cultural reparation rather than a campaign about one.

one_liner: Carnival's biggest stage becomes a Yanomami protest song.
search_doc:
Carnival's biggest stage becomes a Yanomami protest song.
Mechanisms: cultural-moment-via-music, carnival-parade-as-media, indigenous-cause-amplification, samba-school-collab, protest-as-spectacle
Idea: To raise awareness and bring visibility to the Yanomami fight against illegal gold cartels, we created HUTUKARA. A musical narrative with built created the biggest spectacle on the planet — the Rio de Janeiro carnaval. To do so, we joined forces with a samba school in Rio called Salgueiro, in their effort to take this universal song based on what is happening to the Yanomami.
Insight: The Rio de Janeiro Carnaval is commonly referred to as 'the biggest spectacle on the planet'. With a live audience of 100,000 people and TV coverage that reaches over 200M people across Brazil, it became the perfect stage to force Brazil to happen and learn of what is happening to the Yanomami.

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR MUSIC/BRONZE/Hutukara - Salgueiro & Urihi Yanomami (Academicos Do Salgueiro, Andarai _ DM9, Sao Paulo).jpg

extracted.tagline: Bringing the Yanomami fight for survival to the biggest spectacle on earth.
extracted.context: The Yanomami people are an Indigenous group that has existed from the depths of the Brazilian Amazon. In the heart of the Amazon forest, they live in a gold rich area, which has been invaded by illegal gold miners that pollute the rivers with mercury, bring diseases, hunger and violence. The Yanomami are facing extinction at the hands of the mining cartels of the Brazilian government. It still happens to be away from the media and the main Brazilian population making it harder to mobilise their cause and to generate a land outcry.
extracted.insight: The Rio de Janeiro Carnaval is commonly referred to as 'the biggest spectacle on the planet'. With a live audience of 100,000 people and TV coverage that reaches over 200M people across Brazil, it became the perfect stage to force Brazil to happen and learn of what is happening to the Yanomami.
extracted.idea: To raise awareness and bring visibility to the Yanomami fight against illegal gold cartels, we created HUTUKARA. A musical narrative with built created the biggest spectacle on the planet — the Rio de Janeiro carnaval. To do so, we joined forces with a samba school in Rio called Salgueiro, in their effort to take this universal song based on what is happening to the Yanomami.
execution: Original samba-school anthem 'Hutukara' performed at Rio Carnival
Carnival parade by Acadêmicos do Salgueiro built around the Yanomami story
Music release across streaming platforms
Earned national and international press

inferred.one_liner: Carnival's biggest stage becomes a Yanomami protest song.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Channelled Brazil's most-watched cultural spectacle into a Yanomami advocacy moment, hitting 200M views, 280M reach and 8,600+ press mentions while putting illegal mining on the national agenda.

visual.style_description: Dense red-and-black editorial layout dominated by carnival parade photography — feathered Yanomami-inspired costumes in formation and a close-up of a face in headdress — with the 'HUTUKARA' wordmark anchoring the centre and three text columns (Problem, Context, Idea) wrapped around press screenshots and metric blocks.

idea.rationale: Hijacking the Rio Carnival samba-school parade — Brazil's largest live music spectacle — as the vehicle for a Yanomami survival anthem turns an entertainment institution into a protest stage. The idea is culturally precise to Brazil: the samba-school 'enredo' format is the national tradition for telling stories of national importance through music and parade, so the cause-music fit is genuine rather than borrowed. It is music-native because Acadêmicos do Salgueiro composed and performed an original samba enredo, not a celebrity cameo.

strategy.rationale: Acadêmicos do Salgueiro is one of Rio's oldest and most prestigious samba schools and Urihi Yanomami is the Yanomami people's health-rights association, so a partnership between them around an indigenous-survival narrative is exact mission-fit for both — neither party is borrowing purpose. The context is data-anchored in verifiable national reporting (illegal gold mining, mercury river pollution, disease and violence against the Yanomami) and frames a documented human-rights crisis rather than a soft-cause claim. The choice of Carnival as 'the perfect stage' is justified by its 100,000-strong live audience and 200M-reach TV broadcast — Brazilian-native cultural infrastructure for mass mobilisation.

execution.rationale: Orchestration runs from samba-enredo composition with the school, to full parade execution with feathered Yanomami-inspired costumes, to streaming release and earned national/international press pickup. The parade itself is the centrepiece — feature-length cultural performance, not a 30-second spot. Channel detail beyond the Carnival broadcast and the streaming release is thin: paid amplification, social distribution, and partner activation phases are not documented on the board, and press evidence is limited to two outlets shown.

impact.rationale: The 200M views / 280M reach / 50M social impressions and 8,600+ press mentions figures are entrant-reported and unaudited, and the 200M number appears to conflate Carnival's general broadcast reach with campaign-specific viewership — 'X% of what?' applies, since Salgueiro's parade is one of many at Carnival. The implied business outcome for an indigenous-rights cause is policy or behaviour change (mining enforcement, government action, public-opinion shift), and the board does not evidence any specific policy or attitude movement attributable to the parade. The cultural visibility is real but the case rests on share-of-voice rather than measured advocacy outcome.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because Salgueiro's Yanomami samba-enredo is a culturally precise use of Brazil's biggest music stage for a documented humanitarian crisis, but the impact case rests on broadcast-reach claims rather than measurable policy or behaviour change.

one_liner: McDonald's writes a song giving Gen-Z permission to not smile.
search_doc:
McDonald's writes a song giving Gen-Z permission to not smile.
Mechanisms: brand-promise-inversion, original-track-as-the-idea, gen-z-targeted-pop, cultural-moment-via-music
Idea: McDonald's, which had always prioritised smiles, delivered a message to Generation Z through music. The new song featured young popular artists, casting models who are not necessarily perfect, accompanied by Generation Z Collaboration Week x Not Smiling Hot Me, McDonald's ran an anti-bookbuying popular among Generation Z, was cool for the individuality. We to know he his authentic lifestyle as a family. Drinking up for the role being authentic to your true self.
Insight: Gen-Z resents being told to perform happiness; McDonald's wins them back by releasing a song that explicitly tells them they don't have to smile.

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR MUSIC/BRONZE/No Smiles - McDonald_s (TBWA_Hakuhodo, Tokyo).jpg

extracted.tagline: This song brought true smiles to Gen-Zs.
extracted.context: McDonald's, which had always prioritised smiles, delivered a message to Generation Z through music. It's easy not to have a smile and to work authentically. The most important […] every years and spread rapidly. Change is brewing in McDonald's Japan, no matter how the trends […] sweets and friendly smiles for everyone.
extracted.idea: McDonald's, which had always prioritised smiles, delivered a message to Generation Z through music. The new song featured young popular artists, casting models who are not necessarily perfect, accompanied by Generation Z Collaboration Week x Not Smiling Hot Me, McDonald's ran an anti-bookbuying popular among Generation Z, was cool for the individuality. We to know he his authentic lifestyle as a family. Drinking up for the role being authentic to your true self.
execution: Original song 'No Smiles' targeted at Gen-Z
Music video featuring young artists and non-smiling models
TikTok-style portrait gallery of fans not smiling
Collaboration with Billboard

inferred.insight: Gen-Z resents being told to perform happiness; McDonald's wins them back by releasing a song that explicitly tells them they don't have to smile.
inferred.one_liner: McDonald's writes a song giving Gen-Z permission to not smile.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reversed McDonald's signature smile by releasing a Gen-Z-targeted pop track celebrating not smiling, generating 36M views in three months and a 130% lift in brand love among Gen-Z in Japan.

visual.style_description: Retro-pop poster of a young female McDonald's crew member on red holding a tray, lit like a J-pop album cover; hand-drawn yellow 'No Smiles' wordmark dominates; text blocks on the right cover ISSUE, IDEA, RESULT, with a grid of TikTok fan portraits along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Inverting McDonald's signature brand promise — the smile — with an original Japanese pop track titled 'No Smiles' that gives Gen-Z permission to drop the performance of happiness is a clean brand-promise reversal lodged inside a music format. The cultural-symbol subversion is the central creative move: the brand whose name is synonymous with the smile says smiling is optional. Cast and lyric choices that celebrate non-perfect, non-smiling Gen-Z faces extend the inversion across the visual layer.

strategy.rationale: McDonald's Japan is the Japanese arm of the global QSR chain whose long-running positioning is centred on 'smile' as a brand asset (most famously 'Smile — free of charge' in Japanese menus). Releasing a song that explicitly removes that requirement is a calibrated category-leadership move for a Gen-Z audience that resents performative cheer, and the brand has the institutional authority to disclaim its own promise. The 'Gen-Z resists forced positivity' insight is plausible but presented as cultural assertion rather than research — no Japan-specific Gen-Z sentiment data is cited.

execution.rationale: Orchestration includes an original song, a music video with non-perfect models, a Billboard partnership, and a TikTok-style fan portrait gallery, suggesting a coherent Gen-Z-targeted multi-platform release. Channel choice fits the audience (TikTok, music streaming, Billboard tie-in) but the detail is thin: phase logic, paid media, partner artists' names, and amplification strategy are not documented on the board. The board's own copy reads fragmentarily, which limits how much execution credit can be granted.

impact.rationale: 36M views in 3 months and 100,000+ new fans are absolute (entrant-reported) numbers and a reasonable music-engagement signal. The 130% Brand Love among Gen-Z and 115% of applicants figures are unanchored ('130% of what baseline?', 'applicants for what?') and read as soft. The implied business outcome for McDonald's Japan is Gen-Z customer-base growth or visit frequency, and the board substitutes a brand-love proxy without disclosing the baseline or absolute customer counts.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the brand-promise inversion ('the smile brand tells you not to smile') is a precise Japan-cultural move credibly built around an original Gen-Z track, but the impact metrics are unbased percentages and the business outcome is a brand-love proxy rather than commercial change.

one_liner: A football anthem becomes a region's cultural artefact.
search_doc:
A football anthem becomes a region's cultural artefact.
Mechanisms: original-track-as-the-idea, artist-collab-deep, regional-culture-amplification, club-anthem-as-medium
Idea: The artist C.Tangana, and his team, honored Galicia researching and soaking up on local popular culture: its music, its authors and its people. After months of work and the collaboration of artists related to local culture, the piece is delivered, combining the essence of Galician tradition with contemporary aesthetics.
Insight: A football club's centenary cannot be marked by another commercial spot — it must enter the cultural canon as a piece of regional music that fans adopt as their own.

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR MUSIC/GOLD/Oliveria Dos Cen Anos - Real Club Celta (Real Club Celta, Vigo _ Visit Little Spain, Madrid).jpg

extracted.tagline: A historic club celebrates its centenary creating culture through a football team's anthem.
extracted.context: In 2023, Real Club Celta reached its centenary year, a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity to reach out to an entire fanbase and strengthen the club's Galician identity and roots. The objective was to create an anthem to finds its place amongst Galician culture, going far beyond football. The anthem was presented in video clip format, looking to portray Galicia's rich landscapes and represent the club's fans.
extracted.idea: The artist C.Tangana, and his team, honored Galicia researching and soaking up on local popular culture: its music, its authors and its people. After months of work and the collaboration of artists related to local culture, the piece is delivered, combining the essence of Galician tradition with contemporary aesthetics.
execution: Original Galician-language anthem song produced with artist C. Tangana
Music video portraying Galician landscapes and fans
Centenary stadium activation and season-ticket campaign
Earned editorial press across global culture outlets
metrics: 2 million views in the first 10 days after launch
Waiting list for the first time in the club's history

inferred.insight: A football club's centenary cannot be marked by another commercial spot — it must enter the cultural canon as a piece of regional music that fans adopt as their own.
inferred.one_liner: A football anthem becomes a region's cultural artefact.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Earned recognition from The Guardian as 'might be the most artistically ambitious football anthem ever', drove a sold-out centenary season-ticket campaign and a first-ever waiting list at Celta with 6M streams and 4M views.

visual.style_description: Hero portrait of artist C. Tangana on a dark moody background, with bold white serif typography stacked on the left and a vertical press-quote column on the right; metric blocks ('Sold Out', '+6M', '+4M') anchor the bottom-left, finishing with a horizontal press-logo strip.

idea.rationale: Marking a football club's centenary with a Galician-language anthem co-written with C. Tangana — built from local musicology research rather than crowd chants — turns the brand moment into a piece of regional folk-pop that lives outside the stadium. The idea reframes the football anthem as cultural artefact, not match-day jingle, which is rare for the format. It is music-native because the brand commissioned a genuine original composition delivered as artist work, not a brand-soundtrack remix.

strategy.rationale: Real Club Celta de Vigo is a 100-year-old La Liga football club whose identity is anchored in Galician regional pride, and the commission of a Galician-language anthem researched inside local popular culture is a precise expression of that mission rather than generic celebrity sponsorship. The premise that a centenary cannot be marked by another ad — only by entering the cultural canon — is plausible and the board substantiates fan ownership through a sold-out season-ticket campaign and the club's first-ever waiting list. Soft spot: 'Galician identity strengthening' is asserted but no fan-attitude or membership benchmark is shown.

execution.rationale: The artist-collaboration depth is real: months of musicological research, collaboration with Galician-tradition artists, contemporary-meets-traditional production and a video portraying the region's landscapes and fans. Channels are tightly matched — streaming for the song's life, social for fan adoption, season-ticket campaign for commercial conversion, and earned culture press for canon ratification. Press pickup across The Guardian, NYT, Vogue, Esquire and Billboard amplifies a club-marketing piece into a global music story.

impact.rationale: Sold-out season-ticket campaign and the first-ever waiting list in the club's history is the right business outcome for a football brand — concrete commercial behaviour change, not just impressions. 6M streams and 4M views are absolute numbers and externally verifiable on platform, which strengthens the case relative to percentage-only claims. The Guardian quote that it 'might be the most artistically ambitious football anthem ever' is press evaluation rather than audience data and is treated as a credibility signal, not as proof of scale.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because the centenary commission produced a regionally-rooted Galician anthem that drove a first-ever season-ticket waiting list, evidencing that the music genuinely entered the culture rather than the campaign cycle.

one_liner: A pop song confronts the forwarder, not the victim.
search_doc:
A pop song confronts the forwarder, not the victim.
Mechanisms: original-track-as-the-idea, artist-collab-deep, education-curriculum-insertion, social-stigma-reframing, legislative-advocacy
Idea: We collaborated with Dutch pop star MEAU to deliver our message. She interviewed victims of sharing, writing 'A Piece Of Me' based on their experiences. The song became part of the school curriculum, providing a tool for teachers and parents to start the difficult conversation. The video [...] was launched on more than 2 sound share, media social tools and team of what is happening to the Norwegian.
Insight: Leading Dutch telco KPN wants to make the internet safer. Working with experts, we developed the insight that it's not victims, but those who forward intimate images that are responsible. Some sharing the [...] song and music video on more than 1 sound share, media social tools, parents and teachers could ask to talk about it.

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR MUSIC/SILVER/Piece Of Me - KPN (Dentsu Creative, Amsterdam).jpg

extracted.tagline: A song and music video based on heart wrenching real stories. Don't look at the victim, but to those who pressed forward.
extracted.context: 15% of teens engage in sexting. For a normal, private way of flirting for some. But it also makes them vulnerable to online sex crimes — sextortion, image-based depression and loneliness for victims. Society often blames the victim, but working with experts and family affected, it's clear the focus must be on those who press forward.
extracted.insight: Leading Dutch telco KPN wants to make the internet safer. Working with experts, we developed the insight that it's not victims, but those who forward intimate images that are responsible. Some sharing the [...] song and music video on more than 1 sound share, media social tools, parents and teachers could ask to talk about it.
extracted.idea: We collaborated with Dutch pop star MEAU to deliver our message. She interviewed victims of sharing, writing 'A Piece Of Me' based on their experiences. The song became part of the school curriculum, providing a tool for teachers and parents to start the difficult conversation. The video [...] was launched on more than 2 sound share, media social tools and team of what is happening to the Norwegian.
execution: Original song 'A Piece of Me' by Dutch pop star MEAU based on real victim interviews
Music video integrated into school curriculum as a teaching tool
Launch via national broadcasters and social platforms
Earned coverage and high-profile press quotes ('really powerful and important campaign', Sky News)
metrics: Top 3 hit in the Netherlands on Spotify
183% increase in online conversations around shaming

inferred.one_liner: A pop song confronts the forwarder, not the victim.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Drove sexual image-sharing into Dutch law as a crime under the Sexual Crimes Act, supported by a Top 3 Spotify hit, 10.4M streams and 162M PR impressions, while reframing the cultural conversation around blame.

visual.style_description: Split-face close-up of a young woman as the hero — half lit, half in shadow — with the 'A Piece of ME' wordmark superimposed over her face; left column carries dense body copy and press quotes, right column stacks neon-coloured metric blocks, bottom strip shows music-video film stills.

idea.rationale: An original MEAU pop song written from real victim testimony that shifts shame from the victim to the forwarder is a targeted moral reframe delivered through the format teens actually consume. The 'don't look at the victim, but to those who pressed forward' framing is the central creative move and lands cleanly in lyric form. Integrating the song into the Dutch school curriculum gives it a second life beyond chart cycle, turning a music release into a teaching tool.

strategy.rationale: KPN is the leading Dutch telecommunications operator whose stated mission includes making the internet safer, so funding an anti-sextortion campaign aimed at the perpetrators of image-forwarding is core to category responsibility, not borrowed purpose. The context is data-anchored — 15% of teens engage in sexting, image-based abuse leads to documented harm — and the cited expert collaboration with affected families gives the insight institutional rather than agency-generated weight. The behavioural reframe of accountability from victim to forwarder is a substantive strategic position rather than a tagline.

execution.rationale: Orchestration runs from original-song production with an established Dutch pop artist, to a music video, to national broadcaster and social distribution, to formal insertion into school curricula as a parent/teacher conversation tool. Channels match the Gen-Z target precisely: Spotify and YouTube for cultural penetration, school curriculum for institutional reach, earned press for adult mediator audiences. Sky News pickup and the cited 'really powerful and important campaign' quote indicate the orchestration reached beyond Dutch borders.

impact.rationale: Top-3 Spotify hit in the Netherlands with 10.4M streams is a hard, externally-verifiable chart outcome — the right music-business metric to evidence cultural penetration. The 183% increase in online conversations around shaming is unanchored ('183% of what baseline?') and 162M PR impressions are entrant-reported and unaudited. The legislative outcome implied in the qualitative summary — that image-sharing entered Dutch law as a Sexual Crimes Act offence — would be the strongest possible business outcome for a safer-internet brief, but the board does not establish the causal link explicitly.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the song-as-conversation-tool reframes blame onto forwarders and earns chart traction plus curriculum adoption, but the headline behavioural lift is unbased and the policy-change claim is stated rather than substantiated.

one_liner: A pop hit revives a fading language.
search_doc:
A pop hit revives a fading language.
Mechanisms: original-track-as-the-idea, language-preservation, viral-dance-trend, regional-culture-amplification, platform-shazam-domination
Idea: Pop culture pushes languages forward and keeps them alive and relevant for the new generation. So, instead of making an entire country talk about bringing different languages into the spotlight, we decided to make a whole country sing about it. We launched the 'Sing to Remember' project for Coke Studio India with a Gujarati song — Khalasi. Working with native artists, we created a dance-friendly song that got the entire nation together to hum the Gujarati song. In doing so, we gave the language a global platform with Gujarati making its mark on pop culture in India.
Insight: Languages don't die in textbooks — they die when they stop being sung. Pop charts are the only place a fading language can still recruit Gen Z.

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR MUSIC/BRONZE/Sing To Remember - Coca-Cola (VML, Mumbai).jpg

extracted.tagline: Using the power of music to awaken forgotten languages.
extracted.context: India is a land of many languages, 780 to be precise. This linguistic diversity is at a significant risk of diminishing, with 200 of these languages already lost and 150 more at risk. One such language is Gujarati, a language once spoken by Gandhi is now only spoken by less than 5% of the population.
extracted.idea: Pop culture pushes languages forward and keeps them alive and relevant for the new generation. So, instead of making an entire country talk about bringing different languages into the spotlight, we decided to make a whole country sing about it. We launched the 'Sing to Remember' project for Coke Studio India with a Gujarati song — Khalasi. Working with native artists, we created a dance-friendly song that got the entire nation together to hum the Gujarati song. In doing so, we gave the language a global platform with Gujarati making its mark on pop culture in India.
execution: Original Gujarati-language song 'Khalasi' commissioned for Coke Studio Bharat
Music video featuring native artists
Reels and TikTok choreography fueling viral dance trend
Press and PM-level endorsement amplification
metrics: Most Shazamed song ever in India
The first song ever to beat all the Hindi and English songs on the charts

inferred.insight: Languages don't die in textbooks — they die when they stop being sung. Pop charts are the only place a fading language can still recruit Gen Z.
inferred.one_liner: A pop hit revives a fading language.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned a Gujarati-language track into India's #1 charting song with 5B views and 11.6M reels, earning a PM endorsement and giving an endangered language a mass-cultural revival.

visual.style_description: Cinematic hero portrait of artist Aditya Gadhvi in mid-performance holding a red megaphone against a deep-red bokeh background of speaker cones; the 'Sing to Remember' wordmark sits centred under the Coke Studio Bharat lockup, with body text columns flanking the figure and a horizontal results bar of icons at the foot.

idea.rationale: Reviving an endangered Indian regional language (Gujarati) by commissioning a danceable pop hit ('Khalasi') inside Coke Studio Bharat is a precise cultural-amplification idea — pop charts as a language-preservation channel. The reframe is genuine: language extinction is presented not as a heritage problem but as a Gen-Z-relevance problem, and the cure is a chart hit rather than a documentary. It is music-native because Coke Studio commissions and produces original songs as its product, not as ad placements.

strategy.rationale: Coke Studio Bharat is Coca-Cola India's music platform whose explicit charter is showcasing Indian regional musical traditions and emerging artists, so a Gujarati-language commission is exact platform-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The context is data-backed (India has 780 languages, 200 already lost, 150 at risk, Gujarati spoken by under 5% of population) and gives the campaign substantive cultural justification. Soft spot: the 'Gujarati was once spoken by Gandhi, now under 5% of population' framing is intuitively true but the 5% figure is not sourced on the board.

execution.rationale: Orchestration combines artist commission with native Gujarati performers, music video production, Reels/TikTok choreography seeding a viral dance trend, and earned amplification through national press and a Prime Minister shoutout. Channel choice matches the audience: Spotify and Shazam for music discovery, Reels/TikTok for the dance-driven virality engine, broadcast/press for cultural ratification. Phase orchestration around the dance trend is the load-bearing execution mechanism.

impact.rationale: 5 billion views and 11.6 million reels/TikToks created are entrant-reported and unaudited — 'X% of what?' applies — though 'most Shazamed song ever in India' and 'topped Spotify's Global Viral List' are externally-verifiable platform-ranked outcomes that strengthen the case. 'First song ever to beat all Hindi and English songs on the charts' is a verifiable chart-position claim. The implied business outcome for a Coca-Cola music platform is brand affinity and Coke Studio listener growth, and the board does not connect the song's success to specific Coke Studio platform or product metrics.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the pop-chart-as-language-revival idea is genuinely on-mission for Coke Studio Bharat and produced verifiable chart and Shazam outcomes, but headline reach is unaudited and the brand-business connection to the music success is not established on the board.

one_liner: The spreadsheet becomes the music video.
search_doc:
The spreadsheet becomes the music video.
Mechanisms: platform-jailbreak, media-as-product, b2b-craft-stunt, music-tech-platform
Idea: Spreadbeats. A never-been-done-before, ear- and ear-opening way to prove the power of combining video and audio to a medium perfectly tailored to its audience. We partnered with Coachella headlining artist John Summit and coded a four-minute music video into the actual spreadsheet itself. Spotify's salesforce sends its file clients, turning rows and columns into a canvas for swaying to the show into the file was opened.
Insight: Media planners live in spreadsheets. Every year, the Spotify Advertising salesforce sends out thousands of spreadsheets with media plan proposals to potential clients. So, to win over media planners, we brought Spotify's video ad capabilities and its ROI superiority to the place where they spend the most time considering ad buys: their lowly spreadsheets.

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR MUSIC/SILVER/Spreadbeats - Spotify (FCB New York).jpg

extracted.tagline: The first music video entirely coded and experienced in Excel.
extracted.context: In 2023, Spotify launched new video ad units. But there's a problem: media planners and advertisers still consider us as just an audio platform. And audio only commands 2% of the ad spend, despite ads reaching out for proposals, they often request media plans without earmarks for audio ads. That's a shame, because audio ads combined with video on Spotify are 2.2 times more effective than audio ads alone.
extracted.insight: Media planners live in spreadsheets. Every year, the Spotify Advertising salesforce sends out thousands of spreadsheets with media plan proposals to potential clients. So, to win over media planners, we brought Spotify's video ad capabilities and its ROI superiority to the place where they spend the most time considering ad buys: their lowly spreadsheets.
extracted.idea: Spreadbeats. A never-been-done-before, ear- and ear-opening way to prove the power of combining video and audio to a medium perfectly tailored to its audience. We partnered with Coachella headlining artist John Summit and coded a four-minute music video into the actual spreadsheet itself. Spotify's salesforce sends its file clients, turning rows and columns into a canvas for swaying to the show into the file was opened.
execution: Four-minute music video coded entirely into an Excel spreadsheet
Original track by John Summit (Coachella headliner)
Spotify salesforce distributed the file directly to media planners
Excel cells turn into pixels that animate to the music
5 million data points turned into art
All within the Excel file of 16MB
Built-in Excel functions and cutting-edge visuals
Inviting media planners to talk about Spotify's video as units
Personalised pop-ups to talk to recipients
metrics: 5 million data points turned into art

inferred.one_liner: The spreadsheet becomes the music video.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Coded a four-minute John Summit music video entirely inside an Excel spreadsheet sent to media planners — proving Spotify's audio-plus-video ROI inside the exact tool used to allocate their budgets.

visual.style_description: Acid-green pixel-art board mimicking an Excel grid; hero is a laptop showing the spreadsheet music video with magenta and lime cells forming an abstract figure; left column carries 'THE CHALLENGE', 'THE CONTEXT', 'THE IDEA' text blocks; thumbnail strip of execution screens at the bottom.

idea.rationale: Coding a four-minute John Summit music video natively into an Excel spreadsheet — built from 5M data points across 16MB — is a B2B craft stunt that turns the media planner's own tool into the demo of Spotify's video ad capability. The platform jailbreak is precise: cells become pixels, animation is timed to the track, and the file arrives inside the buyer's working environment instead of a deck. It is music-native because an original commissioned Coachella-headliner track is the engine of the demo, not a found library cue.

strategy.rationale: Spotify is the world's largest audio-streaming platform whose 2023 business problem was getting media planners to allocate video budget — audio still commands only 2% of ad spend despite Spotify's audio-plus-video proof of 2.2x effectiveness. Targeting media planners inside the spreadsheet is exact mission-fit for an ad-tech business expansion, not a brand-equity play, and the cited audio-plus-video efficacy stat anchors the case in Spotify's own measurement. The insight that planners 'live in spreadsheets' is observationally true but presented as cultural rather than research-backed.

execution.rationale: The Spotify salesforce directly distributes the file to its real target list, with personalised in-spreadsheet pop-ups inviting follow-up conversations about Spotify video units — a closed-loop B2B activation rather than an open-web campaign. The engineering craft (5M data points, native Excel functions, animation timed to music) is the orchestration. Channel choice is exceptionally narrow and accurate: it ignores consumer media entirely and goes straight to the desks where ad budget is decided.

impact.rationale: The board carries almost no impact data — no documented incremental Spotify video bookings, no campaign-conversion rate, no number of planners reached, no revenue attribution — and the implied business outcome (lift in video-ad spend allocation) is the precise metric a B2B sales tool exists to move. Cited 'metrics' are file-construction inputs (5M data points, 16MB) rather than outcomes. Press coverage is limited to a single David Foster LinkedIn post, which is industry-circle endorsement rather than earned reach proof.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the spreadsheet-as-music-video is a genuinely original B2B platform-jailbreak inside the exact tool planners use to allocate budget, but the impact case is hollow — no documented revenue, bookings or planner-conversion proof.

one_liner: The gift shop IS the museum's reopening.
search_doc:
The gift shop IS the museum's reopening.
Mechanisms: e-commerce-as-rescue, shoppable-music-video, artist-collab-deep, cultural-institution-revival, purpose-driven-product
Idea: Turn a closed museum into a shoppable music-video tour: Liniker performs through its corridors, every artwork is a clickable product on Mercado Libre, and ticket-like sales fund the museum's reopening.
Insight: Half of a museum's revenue comes from souvenir shops — so the gift shop, not the gallery, is the lever to reopen a closed institution.

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR MUSIC/SILVER/The Forbidden Gift Shop - Mercado Libro (GUT sao Paulo).webp

extracted.tagline: How Mercado Libre took 1 million new visitors to a closed museum and raised money to reopen it.
extracted.context: Prejudice and intolerance from the conservative government shut down the Museum of Sexual Diversity. Nearly bankrupt, it urgently needs funds to reopen. So, we launched the museum's first-ever online shop on Mercado Libre. To kickstart it, we teamed up with singer Liniker for an interactive video tour: simply click to explore artworks, meet the artists, and shop at the museum's official gift shop.
execution: Interactive music-video tour hosted by Brazilian singer Liniker through the closed Museum of Sexual Diversity
First-ever Mercado Libre online shop for the museum's official souvenir products
Clickable artworks within the video that linked straight to merchandise purchase
Earned editorial and culture-press coverage

inferred.insight: Half of a museum's revenue comes from souvenir shops — so the gift shop, not the gallery, is the lever to reopen a closed institution.
inferred.idea: Turn a closed museum into a shoppable music-video tour: Liniker performs through its corridors, every artwork is a clickable product on Mercado Libre, and ticket-like sales fund the museum's reopening.
inferred.one_liner: The gift shop IS the museum's reopening.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Raised funds and reach for a politically shuttered LGBTQ+ museum by turning its souvenir shop into a Liniker music-video tour, selling out in 30 minutes and delivering 550M impressions and 5M gift shop visitors.

visual.style_description: Hero scene of singer Liniker walking through a dark-blue staged museum corridor with backlit artwork frames, mimicking an interactive shoppable experience; the 'Forbidden Gift Shop' wordmark spans the top with white blocks censoring 'The'; bottom bar shows the four headline metrics on a yellow strip.

idea.rationale: Turning a closed-by-government LGBTQ+ museum into a Liniker-hosted shoppable music-video tour — where every clickable artwork becomes a Mercado Libre product funding the reopening — fuses e-commerce mechanics with music performance and political resistance in a single click. The idea is structurally elegant: the gift shop becomes the museum's revival vehicle, and the music video becomes the gift shop. It is music-native because Liniker's performance is the centre of the format, not a celebrity overlay.

strategy.rationale: Mercado Libre is Latin America's largest e-commerce platform whose brand mission is democratising commerce access, so building the first online shop ever for a politically shuttered cultural institution is a precise expression of that mission rather than borrowed advocacy. The cited insight — that gift-shop revenue is roughly half a museum's income — is plausible and explains the lever choice, though no specific industry source is cited on the board. The political context (the Museum of Sexual Diversity closed by a conservative Brazilian government) is verifiable and gives the campaign a substantive cause-justification rather than tokenism.

execution.rationale: Orchestration combines artist commission (Liniker, an established Brazilian queer pop figure), interactive video production, and Mercado Libre's e-commerce stack into a single shoppable experience hosted on the platform itself. The phase logic — performance launches the shop, sell-out funds the reopening — closes the commercial loop tightly. Channel detail beyond the platform-hosted video is thin: paid amplification, partner press and offline activation phases are not documented on the board.

impact.rationale: Sold-out in under 30 minutes is the right business outcome for an e-commerce-as-rescue campaign — a hard commercial behaviour, not a surrogate. 700K performance viewers and 5M gift-shop visitors are absolute (entrant-reported) numbers, and 550M impressions is claimed but unaudited and best treated as directional. The board does not disclose how much money was actually raised or whether the museum has reopened, which is the deciding business outcome the campaign exists to deliver.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the gift-shop-as-music-video genuinely fuses commerce and performance to fund a political cause and sold out in 30 minutes, but the central outcome — funds raised and museum reopened — is not quantified on the board.

one_liner: Vinyl records you can watch as much as listen to.
search_doc:
Vinyl records you can watch as much as listen to.
Mechanisms: physical-format-reinvention, vinyl-as-canvas, artist-collab-deep, media-as-product, music-meets-design
Idea: To celebrate the spirit of collaboration and inspired by the growing appetite for nostalgic vinyl culture, Coke Studio transformed the analog format into a tactile, watchable music canvas — Video Vinyl. Each layer of vinyl is hand-illustrated by a Coke Studio artist, telling the story of the track they collaborated on.

full_record.file_path: 2024/ENTERTAINMENT LIONS FOR MUSIC/BRONZE/Video Vinyl - Coca - Cola (Ogilvy Singapore).jpg

extracted.context: Coke Studio leverages teen creators, platforms and music from past Coca-Cola eras to make music and create original music tracks for fans.
extracted.idea: To celebrate the spirit of collaboration and inspired by the growing appetite for nostalgic vinyl culture, Coke Studio transformed the analog format into a tactile, watchable music canvas — Video Vinyl. Each layer of vinyl is hand-illustrated by a Coke Studio artist, telling the story of the track they collaborated on.
execution: Hand-illustrated transparent vinyl records, each layer by a different Coke Studio artist
Special-edition vinyl drops distributed to fans, retail and live concerts
Companion playable music on Spotify
Earned press across music and design outlets

inferred.one_liner: Vinyl records you can watch as much as listen to.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reinvented the vinyl record as a visual collaborative artefact across Coke Studio artists, earning design and music press pickup including Billboard, Nylon, designboom and Esquire as proof of cultural and craft resonance.

visual.style_description: Dark navy backdrop with the hero showing three stacked, brightly illustrated transparent vinyl discs at angled perspective, each layered with mandala-like artwork in red, yellow, blue and pink; left column carries Context/Idea/Execution/Structure text, top bar holds five short press quotes, bottom strip shows production photos and a Spotify card.

idea.rationale: Reinventing the vinyl record as a transparent multi-layer canvas — each layer hand-illustrated by a different Coke Studio artist telling the collaboration story — is a clever physical-format reinvention that fuses music and design craft. The idea reads as a collectible object rather than an ad, and the layering metaphor sits naturally on 'collaboration'. The mechanic is more design-craft than music-craft innovation: the music itself exists separately on Spotify, and the vinyl carries the brand story.

strategy.rationale: Coke Studio is Coca-Cola's global music platform whose mission is showcasing artist collaboration, so a physical artefact that visualises layered artist contribution is on-platform rather than borrowed purpose. The context paragraph on the board is thin — it asserts that Coke Studio works with creators and platforms but offers no substantive insight, market data, or specific business question that the vinyl artefact answers. The strategic case rests on craft fit rather than a sharply defined brand problem.

execution.rationale: Production craft is the load-bearing layer: hand-illustrated transparent vinyl discs, special-edition drops to fans and retail, companion playback on Spotify, and earned press across music and design outlets (Billboard, Nylon, designboom, Esquire). The artefact is genuinely tactile and the multi-artist collaboration is real. Phase orchestration beyond the object-drop is thin: no documented launch event, retail partner, paid amplification or sustained content programme on the board.

impact.rationale: The board carries no quantified outcome data — no streaming numbers, no sales, no fan engagement, no commercial result — which is the most serious gap for a Music entry where streaming/chart/engagement evidence is the category's checklist item. Press across Billboard, Nylon, designboom and Esquire is an unaudited claim but the breadth across both music and design outlets is a credible signal of craft resonance. The implied business outcome (Coke Studio audience or platform growth) is not addressed on the board.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the transparent multi-layer vinyl is a tactile design-craft object that earned music and design press pickup, but it is more visual-design than music-native craft and the board carries no quantified business or music-engagement outcome.

inferred.idea: Use AI to revive Brazil's most beloved singer Elis Regina and pair her with her real daughter Maria Rita in a duet driving two VW Kombis from different eras — a literal generational duet for VW's 70th anniversary.
inferred.one_liner: Mother and daughter, vintage and modern Kombi, one duet.
inferred.qualitative_summary: AI-resurrected Brazilian icon Elis Regina to duet with her living daughter inside two VW Kombis, generating 50M organic views, 2 billion impressions and ranking as one of 2023's most viral Brazilian campaigns.

visual.style_description: Cinematic film-still of two women driving in mirrored close-ups inside vintage and modern VW Kombis on the same desert road; large 'GENERATIONS' title across the middle; dense text columns on the left, metric tower on the right, behind-the-scenes thumbnails in the middle, and a heavy press logo strip at the bottom.

idea.rationale: Using AI to revive Brazilian icon Elis Regina for a duet with her own living daughter Maria Rita inside vintage and modern VW Kombis is a literal generational-bridge idea for VW's 70th anniversary — past meets present, mother meets daughter, old Kombi meets new. The execution moment is undeniably emotional and the brand-meaning fit (generational continuity) is exact. The mechanic, however, is the now-controversial AI-resurrection format and provoked significant ethical debate in Brazil about consent for deceased artists' likenesses, which capped the jury's enthusiasm.

strategy.rationale: Volkswagen do Brasil is the Brazilian arm of the German automaker celebrating 70 years of local manufacturing, and the Kombi is its most culturally-loaded Brazilian product line, so a generational-duet story across two Kombi eras is direct mission-fit for an anniversary brief. The premise that quality and innovation have linked generations of VW is brand-coherent but conventional anniversary positioning. The insight rests on emotional inevitability rather than substantive cultural research, and the board does not address the consent or ethical debate the AI revival generated.

execution.rationale: Orchestration includes a Sunday-morning broadcast TV film, an AI-driven recreation of Elis Regina, a 'Making Of' content layer, and broad cross-media earned coverage. Channel choice is appropriate for the broad Brazilian-family target: broadcast TV for the launch moment, social and press for amplification. The AI craft is the load-bearing technique but is now common rather than novel, and the orchestration does not contain a music-native phase beyond the film itself — no concert, streaming release of the duet as a standalone music product, or artist-led extension.

impact.rationale: 50M organic views, 24h X trend topic, and 2M searches for 'Elis Regina' are absolute or platform-verifiable signals of cultural penetration. The 2 billion impressions figure is entrant-reported and unaudited; '#1 in top 14 trends' and '#4 most viral campaigns of 2023' are external rankings that strengthen the case directionally. The implied business outcome for a VW anniversary campaign is brand-affinity lift, dealership traffic or sales among the Brazilian family target, and the board does not connect the viral reach to any commercial metric or attitude shift specific to VW.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the AI-resurrected Elis Regina duet delivered an undeniable cultural moment perfectly fit to a 70th-anniversary brand-meaning story, but the mechanic is now common, the consent debate it generated is unaddressed, and the impact case rests on reach rather than commercial proof.
